coredata.dc:title: Self-ratiometric photoluminescence carbonized polymer dots with Junas structure for hypochlorite selective sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Chengshuang
Shan, Feishi
Zhu, Yinxia
Mi, Haodong
Liu, Yanhong
Song, Qiao
Wang, Chuanfeng
Wang, Leyong
Wang, Zhouyu
coredata.dc:description: The Janus structure of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) is characterized by an independent yet interdependent core and shell structure, potentially enabling them to function as a self-ratiometric photoluminescence (PL) sensor. However, to our best knowledge, the current lacking understanding and development of the Janus structure of CPDs lead to the unfortunate loss of its position as PL sensors that could have supported life analysis to some extent. Therefore, it is crucial to comprehensively explore the structural characteristics of CPDs and furtherly develop its potential as self-ratiometric sensors. Herein, green and red double-emitting CPDs has been rationally designed and synthesized, incorporating a distinct core-shell structure to create a self-ratiometric sensor for hypochlorite (ClO−). Leveraging the low toxicity and high-water solubility of CPDs, we have successfully achieved exogenous and endogenous ClO− sensing in cells. This approach offers a novel strategy for developing CPDs-based sensors and sets the stage for a more in-depth exploration of the Janus structure of CPDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Dual-emission
Self-ratiometric
Janus structure
Sensor

Syntheis and characterization of core-shell structure of SCPDs
Cytotoxicity and Cell Imaging of SCPDs

We acknowledge Mrs. Yanhong Liu from the Comprehensive Training Platform of Specialized Laboratory, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for the sample analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Janus structure of SCPDs synthesized from CA and TU. (b) TEM images of SCPDs (10 μg/mL) (inset: HRTEM images), and corresponding dots diameter distribution histogram. (c) XRD spectra of SCPDs. (d) XPS spectra of SCPDs. (e) FTIR spectra of SCPDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of SCPDs with different concentrations (1 μg/mL to 50 μg/mL) (inset: linear fitting of absorption values at 532 nm (top) and digital photographs of SCPDs in sunlight (down)); (b) PL spectra of SCPDs with different concentrations (1 μg/mL to 50 μg/mL) under dual-wavelength excitation (415 nm or 545 nm) (inset: digital photographs of SCPDs at 365 nm); (c) Excitation wavelength (360 nm to 600 nm) dependence of SCPDs (10 μg/mL); (d) PL spectra of SCPDs with different pH (λex=545 nm); (e) Schematic diagrams of core-shell dual emission of SCPDs and CEE effect dominated by shell structure.

(a) Effects of various ROS (20 μM) on SCPDs (10 μg/mL) PL (inset: digital photographs of SCPDs and SCPDs + ClO− at 365 nm); (b) The change of PL value of SCPDs at 492 nm and 616 nm; (c) The PL ratios (I492 nm/I616 nm) vs the concentrations of ClO− (0–30 μM); (d) Linearity of the I492 nm/I616 nm ratios with the concentrations of ClO− (7–18 μM).

(a) The effect of ClO− with different concentrations (0 μM to 40 μM) on the absorption spectra of SCPDs (10 μg/mL); (b) XPS spectra of SCPDs and SCPDs (10 μg/mL) + ClO− (20 μM); (c) TEM of ClO− treated SCPDs (10 μg/mL); (d) DLS diagram of SCPDs (10 μg/mL) and ClO− treated SCPDs (10 μg/mL), green column represents hydrodynamic diameter, and red column represents the proportion of large-size areas; (e) Schematic diagram of core-shell structure change of SCPDs after ClO− treatment.

(a) Cytotoxicity of SCPDs with different concentration (0 μg/mL to 40 μg/mL); (b) Exogenous ClO− sensing of SCPDs (top-to-bottom: [ClO−] = 0, 5, 10, and 20 μM), scale bar = 10 μm; (c) PL quantification results for panel (b).

(a) Exogenous ClO− sensing of SCPDs (10 μg/mL) (top-to-bottom: blank, LPS (5 μg/mL) /PMA (5 μg/mL), LPS (5 μg/mL) /PMA (5 μg/mL) + NAC (20 μg/mL)), scale bar = 10 μm; (b) PL quantification results for panel (a).

Schematic diagram of ClO− sensing of CPDs with Janus structure. Strong quenching of its shell-dominated red emission (right), while enhancing the core-dominated green emission (left) after interaction with ClO−, leading to the creation of a self-ratiometric nano sensor.

head.ce:title: Self-ratiometric photoluminescence carbonized polymer dots with Junas structure for hypochlorite selective sensing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: C. Liao and F. Shan contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Janus structure of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) is characterized by an independent yet interdependent core and shell structure, potentially enabling them to function as a self-ratiometric photoluminescence (PL) sensor. However, to our best knowledge, the current lacking understanding and development of the Janus structure of CPDs lead to the unfortunate loss of its position as PL sensors that could have supported life analysis to some extent. Therefore, it is crucial to comprehensively explore the structural characteristics of CPDs and furtherly develop its potential as self-ratiometric sensors. Herein, green and red double-emitting CPDs has been rationally designed and synthesized, incorporating a distinct core-shell structure to create a self-ratiometric sensor for hypochlorite (ClO−). Leveraging the low toxicity and high-water solubility of CPDs, we have successfully achieved exogenous and endogenous ClO− sensing in cells. This approach offers a novel strategy for developing CPDs-based sensors and sets the stage for a more in-depth exploration of the Janus structure of CPDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbonized Polymer Dots with Janus structure as Self-ratiometric ClO− Sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid advancement of nanoscience, the design and fabrication of different optical nano sensors are crucial for enhancing optical nano sensing and imaging (ONSI) technologies [1–4]. Traditional nano sensors used in ONSI technology typically rely on a single 'on' or 'off' signal transduction mode for detection and imaging, which can result in nonspecific and potentially misleading false positive results. To address these challenges, various strategies have been proposing, especially the ratio method is one of the most effective in minimizing nonspecific effects and in providing more accurate and reliable analysis [5–8]. However, a significant number of ratiometric photoluminescence (PL) nanosensors currently rely on multiple emission modules [9–13], underscoring the necessity for simpler, autonomous, and multi-emission PL nanosensors.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are characterized by a special core-shell structure that gives them dual emission properties [14–17], making them a distinctive and innovative material with Janus structure. The core-dominated emission is a result of the band gap transition of a conjugated π domain composed of heteroatom-doped sp2 carbon [18–20]. The remarkable crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect enhances and red-shifts the emission of subluminogens in the polymer shell [15,21–23] provide inherent advantages for constructing ratiometric PL nano sensors. However, current explanations of the sensing mechanisms in ratiometric PL sensors using dual-emission CPDs are frequently lacking in clarity [24], impeding the design and advancement of CPDs-based sensors. In addition, compared to traditional metal quantum dots [25], most CPDs demonstrate excellent biocompatibility [26,27]. As a results, CPDs is an excellent candidate to directly visualize pathological information in living cells with high temporal and spatial resolution.

Sensing and imaging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in vitro and in vivo are crucial for understanding various physiological and pathological processes [28]. Hypochlorite (ClO−), a type of ROS, is commonly found in living organisms. At its normal concentrations, ClO− can protect cells from bacteria or pathogens, however, beyond its normal concentrations, it can alter the structure of nucleic acids, lipids, and other substances, leading to cell apoptosis or necrosis and tissue damage. This, in turn, increases the risk of neurodegenerative diseases and cancer [29]. Therefore, the development of a sensitive ClO− sensor with a high signal-to-noise ratio is essential for qualitative and quantitative detection of ClO− in biological systems.

Herein, a Janus structure of CPDs with dual-emission was developed with a core-dominated emission at approximately 500 nm and a shell-dominated emission around 620 nm based on the classical citric acid (CA)-thiourea (TU) system. Interaction with ClO− results in strong quenching of the shell emission while enhancing the core-dominated emission, leading to the creation of a self-ratiometric nano sensor ( Scheme 1). This enables efficient and selective detection of ClO− with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 5.4 nM. Furthermore, this sensor demonstrates successful exogenous and endogenous sensing of ClO− in cells. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of a self-ratiometric sensor based on CPDs with a special Janus structure for efficient ClO− sensing. The present reserach not only simplifies the construction method of a self-ratiometric sensor based on CPDs with Janus structure, but also provides a clear explanation of the dual-emission mechanism of CPDs and the sensing mechanism of ClO−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, purification and drying of SCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of CA (0.5 g, 2.6 mmol) and TU (1.0 g, 13.1 mmol) was dissolved in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) (10 mL) and then transferred to polyphenol-lined high-pressure reactor. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 6 h in sealed state. The dark brown suspension was cooled to room temperature, transferred to 2 % NaOH aqueous solution (20 mL), stirred thoroughly, and centrifuged to obtain black precipitates. The precipitate was initially washed with 2 % HCl aqueous solution (20 mL), and then with ultrapure water (4 × 5 mL), followed by centrifugation to collect the dark precipitate. Subsequently, the precipitate was frozen at −78 ºC for 24 h and then vacuum dried for 48 h. The resulting purified CPDs were named SCPDs.

The sensor solution was prepared by dissolving SCPDs in Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) buffer. Stock solutions of ClO− were prepared in double distilled water and used as soon as possible. During testing, varying amounts (0, 1, 2, 3, …, 30 μM) of ClO− were added to a sensor solution (10 μg/mL) with a total volume of 2.0 mL for spectral measurements at room temperature. The PL spectra of the solution were recorded with excitation wavelengths of 420 nm and 550 nm, respectively. Both the excitation and emission slit widths were set to 2.5 nm, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was adjusted to 580 V.

Following a 24 h co-incubation of SCPDs (10 μg/mL) with HepG2 cells, the cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, a PBS solution of ClO− with varying concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20 μM) was added for 30 min. After another three PBS rinses, the cells were imaged under dual-channel excitation at 405 nm and 580 nm.

Inflammatory cell models were established based on previous reports [6,30,31]. HepG2 cells were divided into three groups for the experiment. Group I served as the control, which SCPDs (10 μg/mL) were co-incubated with HepG2 cells for 24 h. Groups II and III were treated with SCPDs (10 μg/mL) for 12 h, followed by the addition of 5.0 μL Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) and 5.0 μL Phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) (both at a final concentration of 5 μg/mL) to induce inflammation in the cells. Notably, the Group III was further treated with N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) (20 μg/mL) for 4 h to mitigate inflammation. Subsequently, all three groups of cells were washed three times with PBS and subjected to imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheis and characterization of core-shell structure of SCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: SCPDs with a special core-shell structure were synthesized through the condensation, polymerization, assembly crosslinking, and partial carbonation of CA and TU in DMF [32–34] ( Fig. 1(a)). 2 % NaOH and 2 % HCl solution are primarily utilized in the purification process of SCPDs [32,35], which involves treating the mixture with twice the volume of 2 % NaOH solution to convert the insoluble components into their corresponding sodium salts. Following this treatment, high-speed centrifugation is employed to precipitate the SCPDs-Na+, thereby removing most small molecules and polymers. The subsequent step entails obtaining SCPDs through treatment with 2 % HCl solution, followed by extensive washing with H2O to eliminate NaCl and other impurities. TU, compared to urea, imparts CPDs with the distinctive properties of sulfur elements, leading to easier oxidation of CPDs and thereby enhancing their functional diversity. The SCPDs were found to be well dispersed and circular, with an average size of 2.8 nm as observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1(b)). High-resolution TEM analysis revealed good crystallinity in SCPDs, displaying a graphene-like lattice structure with a crystal plane spacing of approximately 0.21 nm [36] (inset in Fig. 1(b)). The presence of amorphous peaks in the 10–40° is characteristic of CPDs, while sharp diffraction peaks around 26–27° indicate good crystallinity (Fig. 1(c)), specifically pointing to the (002) crystal plane of graphite[37–39]. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements indicated hydrodynamic sizes averaging 310 nm (Fig. 4(d), (e) right, and Figure S6 (a)), reflecting the state of low or uncrosslinked polymer shells[32,40]. Overall, the core-shell structure of SCPDs was effectively characterized using the aforementioned methods.

With X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, SCPDs were found to contain four main elements aside from hydrogen: C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p (Fig. 1(d)). The relative proportions of these elements were determined to be 72.59 %, 8.36 %, 18.73 %, and 0.32 %, respectively, indicating successful doping of N and S, in line with previous studies. The XPS spectra revealed seven major peaks in the high-resolution C1s region, corresponding to C-S (283.3 eV), CC/C-C (284.8 eV), C-N (286.0 eV), C-O (287.1 eV), CN (288.1 eV), and CO (289.0 eV) functionalities (Figure S1 (a)). In the high-resolution N1s spectra, three characteristic peaks representing Pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), Pyridinic N (400.2 eV), and Graphitic N (401.9 eV) were observed (Figure S1 (b)), consistent with typical N-doped CPDs. Additionally, the high-resolution O1s spectra showed three distinct peaks for OC (531.4 eV), O-C (532.4 eV), and OC-O (533.8 eV) [41] (Figure S1 (c)). Despite relatively weak signals, the characteristic peaks of S-C 2p3/2 (163.9 eV) and SC 2p3/2 (165.1 eV) in the S 2p region (Fig. 4(b) down) could still be accurately fitted [42,43]. These findings were further supported by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis (Fig. 1(e)). The peaks around ∼3438 cm−1, ∼1355 cm−1, and ∼700 cm−1 are associated with N-H bonds. The stretching vibration of CO at ∼1640 cm−1 and band at ∼3438 cm−1 and ∼1100 cm−1 is signatures of O-H vibrations [44]. Peaks at ∼2850 cm−1 and ∼520 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration bands of C-H and C-S respectively.

With increasing concentration, the absorption peak gradually rises ( Fig. 2(a)), with a linear absorption value at 532 nm (inset in Fig. 2(a), top), resulting in a rose red appearance of the SCPDs solution (inset in Fig. 2(a), down). Additionally, SCPDs in aqueous solution show sharp emission at 616 nm with an excitation wavelength of 545 nm (Fig. 2(b)). Notably, it is observed that they exhibit dual emission of green and red when investigating the excitation wavelength dependence of SCPDs (Fig. 2(c)). The green emission shows a shift of approximately 100 nm, while the red emission only shifts by 23 nm, indicating a different emission mechanism that aligns with the core-shell structure of SCPDs. Furthermore, SCPDs with varying concentrations maintain stable dual-emission properties under excitation wavelength of 415 nm and 545 nm, which serves as a foundation for the development of self-ratiometric PL sensors.

To investigate the relationship between the core-shell structure and dual emission of SCPDs, we conducted short-term pyrolysis experiments at different temperatures to disrupt the shell structure. Pyrolyzing SCPDs at 120 °C resulted in a decrease in absorption intensity without altering the trend (Figure S4), indicating a partial dissociation of the polymer network in the shell and weakening of conjugation degree and n→π* transition. However, characteristic peaks of SCPDs nearly vanished in the absorption curve, giving way to broader and slower peaks at higher pyrolysis temperatures (180 °C and 220 °C) (Figure S4). The solution color also shifted from rose red to colorless (inset in Figure S4). PL in the green and red regions of SCPDs decreased (Figure S5), with a more pronounced decline in the red region, causing the emission color to shift from pink to green. This suggests that the stability of shell emission is inferior to that of the core state due to the lower stability of the polymer network compared to the highly conjugated core structure. Therefore, the dual emission of SCPDs can be attributed to the integration of the band gap transition of the core-dominated heteroatom-doped conjugated structure and the shell-dominated CEE effect[45–48] (Fig. 2(e)).

The local destruction of the core-shell structure of SCPDs through pyrolysis results in hierarchical PL quenching, offering valuable insights. Our study aims to explore gentler methods to disrupt the core-shell structure of SCPDs, enhancing their dual-emission properties. we are focusing on ROS based on previous research from our group. ROS can readily interact with the polymer shell and carbon core of SCPDs from the exterior to the interior, thereby influencing their respective emission characteristics. Screening six common ROS (H2O2, O2 -·, TBHP, 1O2, ·OH, and ClO−) revealed that only ClO− significantly quenches the red PL of SCPDs, suggesting variations in the oxidation abilities of these ROS ( Fig. 3(a) & (b)). Surprisingly, the green emission from the carbon core is slightly enhanced while the red PL is quenched, improving the sensitivity of the self-ratiometric PL sensor. These changes are visible to the naked eye and the rapid interaction between ROS and SCPDs allows for swift detection in field (inset in Fig. 3(a)).

The concentration-dependent PL response of SCPDs to ClO− was investigated in this study. As the ClO− concentration increased, the PL peak at 616 nm gradually decreased, while the peak at 492 nm showed a slow increase (Fig. 3(c) and Figure S6). The calculated (I492 nm/I616 nm) ratio was found to be proportional to the ClO− concentration within the linear range of 7–18 μM (Fig. 3(d)). The calculated LOD was determined to be as low as 5.4 nM based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. These findings suggest that SCPDs exhibit a sensitive self-ratiometric response to ClO− through various PL signal modes.

The ClO− sensing capability of SCPDs is a topic of significant interest to us. The investigation into the absorption spectra of SCPDs reveals that the characteristic peak at 532 nm gradually decreases as the ClO− concentration increases ( Fig. 4(a)). This peak corresponds to the n→π* transition of the chromophore in the shell structure, suggesting that the conjugated structure or hydrogen bond interaction within the subchromophore of the cross-linked polymer network in the shell structure is disrupted by ClO− [49,50]. Additionally, the characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to π→π* and n→σ* show a slight increase (Fig. 4(a)), possibly due to the oxidation of sulfur to sulfoxide or sulfone in the core structure [29,51] (Fig. 4(e) Left). This oxidation enhances the electronic transition probability, resulting in a color-enhancing effect. XPS spectra further confirm this phenomenon (Fig. 4(b)), with SCPDs exposed to ClO− showing distinct characteristic peaks of sulfoxide and sulfone, along with a noticeable decrease in signal strength of C-S and CS bonds, indicating the oxidation of sulfur by ClO−. The size and lattice of SCPDs were further observed after interaction with ClO− (Fig. 4(c)). Average size of SCPDs is slightly reduced compared with SCPDs (∼0.5 nm), possibly due to the destruction of the highly cross-linked polymer shell closely connected to the core. This destruction reduces the electron density, making it unobservable in TEM. The lattice spacing of SCPDs remains at 0.21 nm, indicating that ClO− primarily affects the shell structure of SCPDs while having minimal impact on the core structure.

The DLS was employed to examine the changes in shell structure of SCPDs after ClO− treatment. Both cases exhibited two sets of peaks in the DLS results, with varying proportions [52,53] (Figure S7). Following ClO− treatment, there was a higher proportion of SCPDs in the large-size region, likely due to depolymerization of the highly cross-linked network structure by ClO−, leading to the release of longer polymer chains and an increase in hydrodynamic diameter (Fig. 4(d) & (e)). Additionally, a slight reduction in average particle size in the small size region was observed, indicating the damaging effect of ClO− on the polymer shell and resulting in a decrease in detected size. It is important to note that multiple repetitions of the experiments were conducted to minimize errors arising from chance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and Cell Imaging of SCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: SCPDs exhibit varying emission intensities in different pH environments. The red emission, primarily influenced by the shell structure, shows instability in strong acid/alkaline conditions but remains stable under physiological pH conditions (Fig. 2(d)). Conversely, the green emission, governed by the core structure, demonstrates consistent performance across the entire pH spectrum, enabling dual-channel biological imaging using SCPDs (Figure S3).

To further explore the application of SCPDs in biological imaging, HL-7702 and HepG2 cells were utilized as cell models to assess the cytotoxicity of SCPDs [30,54], See Cytotoxicity. in SI for details. Remarkably, even at a concentration as high as 40 μg/mL, the cell survival rate remained above 92 % ( Fig. 5(a)), highlighting the exceptional biocompatibility of SCPDs. Subsequent cell imaging revealed that SCPDs could efficiently penetrate cells, disperse within the cytoplasm, and remain excluded from the nucleus, emitting both green and red PL simultaneously. Notably, the red emission appeared more intense than the green emission, consistent with the steady-state spectral findings.

Then, exogenous ClO− sensing of SCPDs was investigated by introducing ClO− (5 μM, 10 μM, and 20 μM) into HepG2 cells incubated with the same concentration of SCPDs (Fig. 5(b) & (c)). The red channel exhibited consistent results with the steady-state spectrum test, gradually weakening in intensity until becoming unrecognizable to the naked eye. Conversely, under the conditions of the green channel, the PL was difficult to identify with the naked eye at ClO− (0 μM, 5 μM, and 10 μM), with a slight increase in intensity observed. However, with the addition of ClO− (20 μM), the green emission was notably quenched, similar to the result of SCPDs pyrolysis. The excessive oxidation by ClO− led to significant damage to both the core-shell structures.

Many diseases are associated with inflammation, which is linked to abnormal ClO− production [6,45,55]. To evaluate the sensing of ClO− in cells, LPS and PMA were used to induce inflammation in HepG2 cells. HepG2 cells were used as a control group and stained with SCPDs. Direct incubation of SCPDs with HepG2 cells resulted in strong red emission but weak green emission. Inflammatory HepG2 cells stained with SCPDs showed enhanced green PL and reduced red PL, indicating ClO− production. To confirm this, inflammatory HepG2 cells were pretreated with NAC, an oxidative free radical scavenger, which resulted in recovered red PL and weakened green PL, similar to the control group. These results suggest that SCPDs can monitor endogenous intracellular ClO− fluctuations independently and simultaneously using different channels without interference. Fig. 6

In summary, SCPDs with Janus structure, self-ratiometric PL sensor, were developed for the quantitative and proportional detection of ClO− with high sensitivity and selectivity. This also provides a new idea for the precise design of the functional "core-shell" structure of CPDs. With low cytotoxicity and high selectivity towards ClO−, SCPDs have been effectively utilized for real-time tracking and imaging of both endogenous and exogenous ClO− in living cells using various PL signal combinations. Janus CPDs sensors, such as the SCPDs discussed here, offer high selectivity, and self-ratiometric quantification, holding great promise for in vitro sensing applications, as well as for studying the role of ClO− in physiological and pathological processes. The present research introduces a potential approach for developing additional Janus CPDs-based nanosensors and sets the foundation for understanding of the core-shell structure of CPDs versus the improved Janus structure in designing CPDs.

Haodong Mi: Investigation. Yanhong Liu: Investigation. Feishi Shan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yinxia Zhu: Investigation. Leyong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhouyu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qiao Song: Investigation. Chuanfeng Wang: Investigation. Chengshuang Liao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1977 and 2022ZYD0048), Science and Technology Innovation Competition Project for Postgraduate Students, Xihua University (YK20240176). We acknowledge Mrs. Yanhong Liu from the Comprehensive Training Platform of Specialized Laboratory, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for the sample analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluating the activity of neuraminidase in bacterial vaginosis microflora and imaging sialic acid on the cell membrane by boron and nitrogen codoped fluorescent carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-responsive ratiometric fluorescent probe for hypochlorite and peroxynitrite detection and imaging in vitro and in vivo

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of efficient multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots with efficient yellow fluorescence emission for detection of hypochlorite and thiosulfate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Gao, Y. Liu, H. Zhang, W. Lu, Y. Jiao, S. Shuang, et al. One-pot synthesis of efficient multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots with efficient yellow fluorescence emission for detection of hypochlorite and thiosulfate, J. Mater. Chem. B. 10 (2022) 8910-8917.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Chengshuang Liao is currently a postgraduate student at Xihua university.
Mr. Feishi Shan is currently a doctoral candidate at Nanjing University.
Yinxia Zhu received her bachelor's degree in Chemistry in 2022 from Xihua university.
Mr. Haodong Mi is currently a postgraduate student at Xihua university.
Mrs. Yanhong Liu is currently working at Sichuan University, and her research interests focus on fluorescence imaging.
Dr. Qiao Song is currently working at Xihua University, and his current research focuses on synthetic chemistry methodology.
Dr. Chuanfeng Wang is currently working at Xihua University, and his current research focuses on fluorescent probes.

Prof. Leyong Wang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2000. Now he is a full professor of Nanjing University, and his current research focuses on supramolecular chemistry and smart materials.

Prof. Zhouyu Wang received his Ph.D. degree from Chengdu Institute of Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2007. Now she is a full professor of Xihua University, and her current research focuses on carbon dots (especially carbonized polymer dots) and organic synthesis.
